planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in well-drained to dry, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; catbrier spreads by both seed and underground rhizomes to form dense, thorny thickets that provide important nesting cover and berry food for wildlife in dry woodland and edge habitats.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun for wildlife habitat restoration; catbrier is a common native greenbrier of dry woodlands and woodland edges throughout eastern North America, distinguished from other Smilax by the glaucous (blue-white) coloring on the underside of the leaves. Like all greenbriers, it is an armed, scrambling vine with stout, curved thorns and tendrils, climbing through shrubs and into tree canopy. The blue-black berries that persist through winter are critically important food for numerous thrushes, mockingbirds, and other fruit-eating birds. Dense catbrier thickets provide nesting cover for ground-nesting birds including bobwhite quail and wild turkey. The young shoots in spring are edible, resembling asparagus in flavor, and have been used as a spring vegetable by indigenous peoples and early settlers. The starchy underground tubers can be processed into a flour-like starch. Primarily a wildlife planting rather than a garden ornamental.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; roundleaf greenbrier is the most common greenbrier of moist woodlands and thicket edges in eastern North America. It forms dense, thorny, impenetrable tangles that are valuable as wildlife habitat but must be sited carefully in gardens.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun for wildlife habitat restoration in naturalistic settings; roundleaf greenbrier is the most widespread and familiar Smilax species of eastern North American woodlands, forming nearly impenetrable thickets of stout, thorny, green-barked stems along forest edges, thicket margins, and disturbed moist areas. The rounded, shiny green leaves with prominent parallel veining are distinctive, remaining deep green through summer before turning a dull yellow in fall. The blue-black berry clusters persist through winter and are among the most important winter food sources for mockingbirds, catbirds, cedar waxwings, bluebirds, and wild turkeys. Dense greenbrier thickets serve as critical nesting habitat, providing protection from predators that avoids entering the thorny tangles. Young shoots are eaten in spring. A foundational wildlife plant for large naturalized areas, woodland restorations, and edge habitats.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full shade; Henry's anise tree is the hardiest of the Chinese Illicium species and one of the few broad-leaved evergreen shrubs that thrives in deep shade in zones 6–9.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full shade; Henry's anise tree is a Chinese evergreen shrub or small tree prized for its ability to thrive in deep shade — one of the relatively few large-growing broad-leaved evergreens that performs well in the dense shade beneath mature trees where most broadleaf shrubs languish. The large, glossy, dark green, willow-like leaves are aromatic when crushed. The flowers are rosy red to deep pink, star-shaped, and appear in late spring — more showy than those of the native Florida anise (I. floridanum) but less fragrant. Named for Augustine Henry, the Irish plant explorer who collected extensively in China in the late 19th century. Somewhat hardier than Florida anise, tolerating temperatures to 0°F in a sheltered spot. An excellent large screening shrub for shaded gardens in the Southeast.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese sycopsis is an uncommon Hamamelidaceae relative that blooms in late winter and provides year-round interest with its holly-like evergreen foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese sycopsis is an uncommon and underused member of the witch-hazel family (Hamamelidaceae) from central China, providing year-round ornamental interest through its dense, holly-like evergreen foliage of dark, glossy, spiny-margined leaves. The late-winter flowers — small, yellowish, without petals — are unspectacular individually but appear in late January or February before most woody plants show any sign of life, providing early-season pollen for queen bumblebees. The compact, dense growth habit makes it an excellent formal evergreen screening shrub or hedging plant. More shade-tolerant than most Hamamelidaceae relatives and particularly well adapted to the woodland garden in zones 7–9. Rarely available in US nurseries but worth seeking from specialist growers for a distinctive, refined addition to the winter garden.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Distylium myricoides (sold as 'Vintage Jade' and related cultivars) has become one of the most popular new evergreen shrubs in the US Southeast for its compact habit, year-round glossy foliage, and exceptional heat and drought tolerance once established.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Distylium is a genus of evergreen shrubs and trees in the witch-hazel family (Hamamelidaceae) from China and Japan, emerging as one of the most important new landscape shrubs for the mid-South and Southeast. The species D. myricoides produces small, glossy, dark green leaves with a neat, compact, spreading habit and unusual winter-to-early-spring flowers — red stamens without petals that create a purplish-red haze across the plant in late winter. Exceptionally drought and heat tolerant once established and resistant to deer browsing. Widely used in current Southeast US landscaping as a low-maintenance foundation plant; cultivars including 'Vintage Jade', 'Linebacker', and the Coppertone series are widely available. Thrives in part shade and full sun with minimal care. Disease and pest resistant.
